full_response:
Step 1.  Write the objective in matrix form and identify the Hessian  
For every quadratic of the form  

       f(x) = ½ xᵀ Q x + cᵀx + d,               (1)  

the gradient is ∇f(x)=Qx+c and the Hessian is the constant matrix H = Q.

In our case  

       f_{A,b}(x) = ½ xᵀ (AᵀA) x − bᵀ A x              (2)

so  
       H = ∇²f_{A,b}(x) = AᵀA.                           (3)

Step 2.  Compute AᵀA  
A = [ [1, 2],  
      [-1, 2] ]

Aᵀ = [ [ 1, -1 ],  
       [ 2,  2 ] ]

AᵀA =  
[ 1·1 + (−1)(−1)     1·2 + (−1)2 ]  
[ 2·1 +  2(−1)       2·2 +   2·2 ]

     = [ 2   0 ]  
       [ 0   8 ]                                    (4)

Thus  

       H = [ 2 0 ; 0 8 ].                              (5)

Step 3.  Eigenvalues of the Hessian  
Because H is diagonal, its eigenvalues are the diagonal entries:

       λ₁ = 2 , λ₂ = 8.                                (6)

Step 4.  Strong convexity constant α  
Definition: f is α-strongly convex iff for every unit vector x,

       xᵀ H x ≥ α.                                    (7)

For any unit vector x, the Rayleigh quotient satisfies

       λ_min(H) ≤ xᵀ H x ≤ λ_max(H).                  (8)

Since λ_min(H)=2, the inequality (7) certainly holds with α=2.  
If we tried any α>2, pick the eigenvector v corresponding to λ₁=2; then vᵀ H v = 2 < α, contradicting (7).  
Hence the largest admissible strong–convexity constant is

       α* = 2.                                        (9)

Step 5.  Smoothness constant β  
Definition: f is β-smooth iff for every unit vector x,

       xᵀ H x ≤ β.                                    (10)

Again by (8) the tightest upper bound is λ_max(H)=8.  
If β<8, take the eigenvector corresponding to λ₂=8 and (10) is violated.  
Therefore the smallest admissible β is

       β* = 8.                                        (11)

Conclusion  
f_{A,b} is 2-strongly convex and 8-smooth, and these constants are the best possible.

\boxed{\text{Strongly convex with }\alpha = 2;\quad\text{smooth with }\beta = 8}

Confidence: 0.84
